course_description: Economics focuses on choices between alternative ways of using resources and the consequences of these choices, which often have to be made in the face of problems associated with the availability of information and limitations to knowledge. These choices concern issues such as what to consume, what to produce, how to produce it, how to organize production between different economic entities, and what prices to charge. Choices taken by individuals need to be coordinated to avoid wasted opportunities, the more so the more that specialization is taking place at the level of the individual, firm or national economy. A major coordinating role is played by price mechanisms, i.e. adjustments in relative prices and the incentives provided by profits and losses. However, much coordination is achieved via the use of planning and institutions. Even with coordination assisted by these devices coordination failures can still occur, such as major slumps in employment and international trade. Economic theory can help to assess when these problems are likely and how they may be dealt with.

requirements.incompatible: ECON2010, or 2400 or 7003 or 7010 or 7011 or MEBS7103
requirements.assumed_background: No background in economics is assumed. However, if you want to do some reading before the course starts, to get a sense of the economic way of thinking, then some good sources would be (on microeconomics) Tim Hardford (2006) The Undercover Economist , Oxford, Oxford University Press, and (on macroeconomics, and the first economics book that the course coordinator read before starting economics) Michael Stewart, Keynes and After (various editions), Harmondsworth, Penguin.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: To provide students with the economic principles which underpin much thinking in other management disciplines such as marketing and strategic management. To demonstrate why managers might benefit from a sound knowledge of economic analysis and techniques. To help students move away from black and white (dualistic) thinking, towards being able to see issues from a range of points of view and to make commitments on the basis of argued cases. To develop skills in identifying which economic tools may be useful in particular contexts and in applying such tools to analyse business problems.

learning_outcomes.description: Employ economic concepts to appraise critically aspects of firms' strategic choices
Demonstrate that they can use economic concepts to conduct analysis rather than merely reporting on economic behaviour
Demonstrate awareness of the relationship between economics, marketing and management
Identify contexts in which mainstream economics concepts will be more useful as analytical tools than those from heterodox economics, and vice versa.
Employ knowledge of a variety of cost and revenue concepts to deal with resource allocation problems
Analyse the possible ramifications of events affecting a firms macroeconomic environment
Evaluate the threats and opportunities that a firm may face due to technological change and the globalization of the world economy
Employ economic concepts to critically appraise corporate press releases and items in the business press

assessment_details.title: Final Exam
assessment_details.task_description:
The final exam will be similar in format to exams set previously for this course and its precdecessor ECON7011, with three equally-weighted sections, each containing three questions from which one must be chosen. The final section will contain questions on macroeonomic issues and international trade. Note that the section on these topics has almost invariably been the one in which students scored least well, so be sure to invest adequate time in reading for this part of the course.

The exam will be in essay format, though some of the essays may have multiple sections.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

assessment_details.title: BEELINE
assessment_details.task_description:
The origins of BEELINE are explored in the 'Born Global' report of the Australian Business Foundation, available via Blackboard. The firm has since been acquired by Hemisphere GPS, whose website is at www.hemispheregps.com. Using you skills in economics and information about the firms from these two sources, you are invited to identify economic factors that affected the emergence of BEELINE and to consider critically choices that its founders made (their choices might stand up to economic scrutiny perfectly well, but they need to be assessed relative to alternatives that could have been chosen instead). Having done this, you should then examine the economic rationality acquisition of BEELINE by Hemisphere GPS from the standpoint of both companies.

Your answers in total should not exceed about 2000 words.

Hand in to the BEL Faculty Resource Centre, Level 1, Colin Clark Building (39), UQ St Lucia.

assessment_details.title: The Global Financial Crisis
assessment_details.task_description: Discuss the view that it is a mistake to believe that a fast and sustained recovery from the current global recession can be achieved by the kinds of 'stimulus packages' that governments around the world have been trying to implement, since it is perverse to try to deal with a crisis that was caused by spectacular growth in the indebtedness of the private sector by spectacularly increasing the indebtedness of the public sector.

learning_resources.required_resources:
Earl, P.E. and Wakeley, T. (2005) Business Economics: A Contemporary Approach , Maidenhead, UK, McGraw-Hill

While we would prefer to teach purely from primary sources, this is difficult in an approach to economics that weaves together in each lecture ideas from many different sources that have emerged over a long period. This textbook covers most of the material from this course in a convenient form. In the list of recommended readings I have tried to indicate key primary sources that inspired it or which develop further key ideas contained in it. I hope you will try to read some of these primary sources rather than merely relying on the textbook. However, note that the textbook's website includes notes on many questions based on previous UQ MBA Business Economics tutorials, along with glossaries of terms and crossword puzzles that enable you to check your understanding of them.

Guidelines for correct referencing techniques can be found in the School of Economics publication Guide for Assignment Presentation , which can be accessed on the web site, www.uq.edu.au/economics

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Introduction/Economic coordination problems (Lecture): (Peter Earl) Core concepts: opportunity cost, supply and demand and the role of incentives. Taxonomy of information and knowledge problems faced by decision makers. Shows how specialization and exchange bring coordination problems both inside and between economic entities. Explores three methods used for avoiding resource wastage via coordination failures: the price mechanism (supply and demand, and the role of the entrepreneur), the visible hand of management/voice mechanism, and cooperation, and the role of market institutions. Examines of the role of the entrepreneur. Readings/Ref: Earl&Wakeley05 (chs 1-3); Leijonhufvud86; Richardson59; Richardson71; Simon59
Opportunity costs and markets (Tutorial): (Peter Earl) Consideration of the following questions:

Opportunity Costs and international trade (Lecture): (Peter Earl) Examines the structure of the balance of payments and the factors underlying the current and capital accounts. Examines strategic responses to changes in exchange rates and contrasts static and dynamic views of comparative advantage in relation to trade policy and globalization. Readings/Ref: Alexander52; Earl&Wakeley05 (sections 14.1-2, 14.7-14.14.); Gray05; Wolfson02

Markets and the Entrepreneur (Lecture): (Neil Kay)Explores the role of relative price adjustments and the forces of supply and demand in directing the allocation of scarce resources. Considers the impacts of price regulation and information problems. Readings/Ref: Earl&Wakeley05 (chapters 2 and 3)

Oil prices (Tutorial): (Neil Kay) We will consider the following questions: 1. Examine how food prices may be affected by changes in oil prices. What factors are likely to cause prices of both food and oil to be unstable?

Contracts and Business Organization (Lecture): (Neil Kay) The economics of vertical integration versus outsourcing; economics of licensing and franchising. Economic consequences of different forms of contracting (classical, relational), for example in respect of reputation and goodwill. The dynamics of business organization through the product life cycle. Readings/Ref: Earl&Wakeley05 (chs 3 and 9); Blois72; Coase37; Earl95 (sections 10.1-10.8, 11.2-11.5 ); Kay93 (part II); Kay97 (chapter 3); Langlois03; Richardson72; Richardson99

The architecture of modern business (Tutorial): (Neil Kay) Examples of tutorial discussion questions: 1. The main stages in the value chain for petroleum products are: exploration for oil deposits, extraction of oil from the ground, transport by tanker or pipeline, refining, delivery in tankers, and retailing at petrol stations. What does the literature on economic analysis of vertical integration lead you to expect about which of these activities an oil company would internalize? For any activities that you expect to be subject to market contracts rather than internalization, indicate the kind of contract that you expect to be involved.

Limiting the Costs of Producing the Product (Lecture): (Neil Kay) Introduction to economists cost concepts and factors affecting them: opportunity costs, fixed costs, variable costs, sunk costs, marginal costs, transaction costs, increasing and decreasing returns, economies of scale and scope, learning curves. Readings/Ref: Earl&Wakeley05 (chs 5, 6, 11); Earl95 (ch. 7); Kay83; Langlois99

E-commerce economics/the future of Mitsubishi (Tutorial): (Neil Kay) We will consider the following questions: 1. Compare and contrast the cost structures of some traditional service providers with those of their modern on-line counterparts (for example, a traditional bookstore versus Amazon.com, a traditional introduction agency versus an Internet dating service, a traditional bank or insurance company versus one that purely operates online). As you do so, examine how the extent of the service provided also differs and consider the implications of these differences in cost structures and service provision for the likely effects of competition on the functioning of the markets in which these firms operate.

Economics of Corporate Growth (Lecture): (Neil Kay) Examines Penroses theory of the growth of the firm and work inspired by it, with an emphasis on Neil Kays analysis of pattern in corporate evolution: the receding managerial limit to firm size, specialization versus diversification, economics of conglomerates, multinational enterprises, economics of mergers, joint ventures, strategic alliances and networks. Readings/Ref: Earl&Wakeley05 (ch. 10); Earl95 (ch. 11); Foss99; Kay97; Langlois03; Penrose59; Pitelis00

Diversification strategies (Tutorial): (Neil Kay) We will consider the following questions: 1. Fisher and Paykel makes whitegoods (washing machines, ovens, refrigerators and dishwashers) but does not make consumer electronics products, unlike Sony, which does not make whitegoods but makes televisions, audio products, mobile phones and laptop computers. However, the Korean firms Samsung and LG make both whitegoods and consumer electronics products, as well as other household appliances such as vacuum cleaners. Use economic theory to discuss how the relative long-term prospects of these firms could be affected by their differences in diversification strategies.

Pricing and competition (Lecture): (Neil Kay) Pricing and output decisions in markets with differing degrees of discretion, ranging from monopoly via monopolistic and imperfect competition to perfect competition. Price discrimination and mark-up/normal cost pricing. Readings/Ref: Earl&Wakeley05 (ch. 7); Andrews49; Davies&Davies84; Earl95 (chapters 8 and 9)

Pricing in context (Tutorial): (Neil Kay) We will discuss the following questions: Do you think the marginalist theory of pricing is more useful than the full cost/normal cost theory for making sense of prices in the market for (a) sports goods and (b) financial services? Explain your reasoning.

Efficiency, productivity and value added (Lecture): (Neil Kay) An examination of factors affecting the firms profit margins with a focus on problems of knowledge and access to distinctive capabilities and the ability to prevent resource owners from capturing rents: motivation versus engineering as determinants of productivity, X-inefficiency and organizational slack, the economics of internal organization, the tacit knowledge problem, Ricardian rents versus monopoly rents. Readings/Ref: Earl&Wakeley05 (chs 6 and 8 and sections 10.8-11.3); Earl95 (sections 7.10-7.12 and 11.7); Foss97; Foss99; Kay93 (parts I and III); Leibenstein66; Peteraf93

Footballers' pay; pricing and organization design (Tutorial): (Neil Kay) We will be considering the following questions: 1. In the summer of 2001 the footballer Ruud van Nistelrooy was bought by Manchester United Football Club from the Dutch football club PSV Eindhoven for £19.5 million. His contract specified that he would be paid around £35,000 - £45,000 per week. To December 2003 van Nistelrooy had scored a record-breaking 97 goals in 118 games. In January 2004 the acting chief executive of Chelsea Football Club was reported to have said that Chelsea would be prepared to buy van Nistelrooy for £60 million if they considered him to be a good investment. The Spanish giants Real Madrid also expressed an interest in securing his services. Later that month Manchester United increased van Nistelrooys weekly wage to around £80,000.
Feedback on First Assignment (Review): Neil Kay will return the first assignment and discuss how well it was handled by members of the class.
The Competitive Process (Lecture): (Neil Kay) An exploration of the economics underlying the changing fortunes of firms, particularly in markets characterised by innovation.

Microeconomics Determinants of a product's sales (Lecture): (Peter Earl) Critically explores the notion of a demand curve, showing the circumstances in which the relationship between price and quantity purchased may change. Discussion of how buyers use particular types of decision rules to cope with problems of information and knowledge and how these problems vary between decision contexts. Readings/Ref: Earl&Wakeley05 (ch. 4); Earl95 (chs 2-4, esp. 72-88); Gigerenzer96

Context and choice (Tutorial): (Peter Earl) We will consider the following questions: 1. Critically appraise the design of the Amazon.com website in the light of material covered in this course 2. Discuss how the context of choice is likely to affect consumer decision-making processes and supplier responses in each of the following cases:

The firms macroeconomic environment (Lecture): (Peter Earl) Takes the economic coordination problem to the level of the economy as a whole, focusing on national income concepts, flows of funds between sectors, saving, investment and the financial system to show how aggregate-level behaviour is not just a simple summation of individual choices and why employment, economic growth rates and price indices fluctuate. Readings/Ref: Earl&Wakeley05 (section 12.1-13.7); Davidson06; Robinson37

Saving, spending and employment (Tutorial): (Peter Earl) We will consider the following questions: Examine the mechanisms by which rising levels of household indebtedness can be financed in economies such as Australia and the US that have chronically low average rates of household saving. What would be the macroeconomic consequences of consumers in such economies deciding on average to reduce their indebtedness?

Speculation and economic stability (Lecture): (Peter Earl) Examines how the modern macroeconomic and international trade environments are prone to be affected by speculation in financial, real estate and foreign currency markets. Contrasts classical and movement trader views of speculation and examines scope for hedging, the layered nature of modern financial systems and stabilizing role of reserve banks. Readings/Ref: Earl&Wakeley05 (sections 13.8, 14.3-14.4); Earl90 (ch. 12); Mehrling99; Whalen01

Interest rates and macroeconomic instability (Tutorial): (Peter Earl) We will consider the following questions: Would you expect macroeconomic instability to be a greater problem nowadays than it was in Keyness time? Explain your reasoning.

Overspill/overview (Lecture): (Peter Earl) We will use this lecture to complete the macroeconomnics section (if necessary), review the course as a whole, and examine what happened in the previous year's final exam for MBA Business Economics

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25122 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

Where an adjustment is made to an accredited program, it is the responsibility of the relevant Faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of the change/s.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course offers a pluralistic coverage of economic theories that are useful in a business environment and their applicability is shown by the use of case studies that do not lend themselves to clear-cut answers. Economics is conventionally taught by focusing on resource allocation problems in a given situation but in this course we focus on how the nature of problems faced by firms varies according to their context. Hence we proceed by focusing on the process of development of a firm from the perception of a market opportunity and creation of a new enterprise through to the economic issues that arise when developing a large, multi-product firm. A major unifying theme is the economic significance of problems of information and knowledge and how they affect solutions to resource allocation problems. This theme leads to an appreciation of links between the economic theory covered in the course and disciplines such as marketing and strategic management, and the agency problems associated with auditing and human resource management. Having examined the development of a business in economic terms we conclude by examining the functioning of the firms national and international economic environment, with an emphasis on speculative choices in both settings and the significance of using particular policies for dealing with problems such as inflation, unemployment and foreign debt.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Postgrad Coursework) graduate attributes:

attributes.attribute: A2 . A broad understanding of the field of study, including how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A3 . A comprehensive and in-depth knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A7 . An appreciation of the link between theory and practice .
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
B5 . The ability to practise as part of an interdisciplinary team .
C2 . The ability to work and learn independently and effectively .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C5 . The ability to formulate and investigate problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
C6 . The abilities and skills that provide a foundation for future leadership roles.
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D4 . The ability to process material and to critically analyse and integrate information from a wide range of sources.
D5 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions using an evidence-based approach .
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E3 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .
E7 . The ability to work effectively and sensitively across all areas of society .
E8 . An understanding of and respect for the roles and expertise of associated disciplines .

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

0-19%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20-44%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

A common cause of students getting this grade on an answer, aside from having done little work or achieved a poor understanding, is a failure to answer the question that was set (for example, if the question says 'explore the relationships between the following concepts', you must explore the relationships between them, not define or describe them), or a failure to attempt all parts of the question. So, read the wording very carefully. 45-49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A basic requirement of being able to think like an economist is demonstrating that you consider the relative costs and benefits of alternative possible choices and the implications of differences in the incentives they offer to decision makers. If you fail to do this, you may have a hard time getting a 4, let alone a better grade, even if you look at the pros and cons of a particular choice from the standpoint of relevant economic theory. 50-64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Note here the phrase 'ability to apply': if your answers primarily report on facts that you have gathered or merely summarize the work of others you will be failing to show what you can do with economic concepts as tools of analysis and you will not score more than a 4. For a 5 of beyond, you must show that you can think like an economist rather than merely displaying abilities in terms of researching facts or what some economists think. 65-74%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

To get beyond a 5, it is helpful to be able to demonstrate that you know what is predicted by relevant economic theory in the context in question and consider how well the predictions match up with facts. 75-84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Integrative thinking that relates concepts from different parts of the course will be highly regarded as will the application of ideas/arguments from reading beyond the prescribed text or lecture notes. 85-100%
late_submission: A late penalty may be applied (up to 1% from your final score per day) unless an extension has been granted previously.
other_assessment_information:
SPECIAL EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a special examination at a later time.

Information about special examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/special-examinations Please read this information very carefully.

All applications for special exams must be submitted within (5) days of the original date of the examination.

End of Semester Examination (SCHOOL EXAM) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with appropriate documentation.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you can find information about supplementary examinations at : http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/supplementary-examinations-2901 Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Special & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (22-24 July).
